item.title: Reaching for the Revolutionary: 2026 Nebraska Baseball.
item.dc:creator: Marissa Monico
item.description:
Husker Baseball has already done the impossible. Two years ago they won the B1G Tournament for the first time and created an atmosphere never before seen around their program. Last year, against all odds, they beat Oregon, the B1G season champion, and UCLA, the future 2025 CWS representative, to take the B1G Tournament crown again. With a slightly above average season in 2025, players like Robby Bolin, Cael Frost and Gabe Swanson helped the team come from behind to take the victory.

This year, the team has already made more of a mark than the last two years combined. Senior shortstop Dylan Carey surpassed the All-Time Career Doubles record, previously set by head coach Will Bolt. New transfer Mac Moyer is second on the B1G leader board for batting average, with Carey securing the No. 5 spot. Sophomore pitcher Carson Jasa is also second on the B1G leader board for most strikeouts, while Gavin Blachowicz is ranked fifth in the ERA standings. The team has so much to be proud of, yet so much to accomplish with the tournament right around the corner. There is history still to be made with the possibility of being the first team to ever have a 3-peat in the B1G Tournament. The only big obstacle: UCLA.

If Nebraska’s season was historic, UCLA’s has been revolutionary. They have topped the NCAA baseball charts all year. With only two losses this year, none have been in-conference, making them the undisputed top dog. Why is that? What makes them so hard to topple? And can Nebraska be the one to do it to make their dreams come true? Let’s see what the hitting tells us.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggnewscale)

b1ghitting <- read_csv("B1G HITTING STATS - Sheet1.csv") 

nu <- b1ghitting |> 
  filter(Name == "Nebraska")
ucla <- b1ghitting |>
  filter(Name == "UCLA") 

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=b1ghitting, aes(x=AVG, y=RBI)) +
  geom_point(data=nu, aes(x=AVG, y=RBI), color="red")+
  geom_point(data=ucla, aes(x=AVG, y=RBI), color="royalblue")+ 
  geom_text_repel(
    data=nu, 
    aes(x=AVG, y=RBI, label=Name))+ 
  geom_text_repel(
    data=ucla, 
    aes(x=AVG, y=RBI, label=Name))+
  labs(
    x="Total batting average", 
    y="Total RBI's", 
    title="UCLA may be ranked No. 1, but Nebraska \n is hitting better overall in the B1G.", 
    subtitle="Nebraska Baseball matches UCLA's domination through their consistent batting.", 
    caption="Source: NCAA | By Marissa Monico"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot", 
    legend.position = "none"
    )

Although UCLA is No. 1 in the polls, Nebraska is No. 1 in batting average. With an average of 0.315, they beat out every other team by a recognizable amount, while UCLA sits with a 0.303. That is great, good for Nebraska. Yet, if you look closely, UCLA has more RBIs. So the next thing you have to think about is what you want to make an argument for. Runs win a the game. That is literally how you win, no one can deny that, but their hitting is not as consistent. There is an case to be made that consistent hitting, that gets you on base, will eventually find equilibrium with your RBIs. All you need is the next hit, and there is officially no better team in the B1G who does that than Nebraska.

There is another side we have not looked at yet: the pitching. When there is an argument to made about the importance of hitting, there is an even bigger one to be made about the importance of pitching. You cannot go anywhere without good pitching. And Nebraska will not beat UCLA without it. Will the starters be able to get the job done?

Code 
neb_ucla_pitching <- read_csv("/Applications/spmc350/Blog/posts/nebraska-baseball-2026-B1G/NEB_UCLA 2026 PITCHING.csv")
uclapitching <- read_csv("UCLA 2026 PITCHING.csv")
nebpitching <- read_csv("NEB 2026 PITCHING.csv")

pitching_stats <- neb_ucla_pitching |> 
  group_by(NAME) |> 
  summarise(
   Strikeouts = sum(SO),
   Walks = sum(BB))

neb_pitching_stats <- nebpitching |> 
  group_by(NAME) |> 
  summarise(
   Strikeouts = sum(SO),
   Walks = sum(BB))

ucla_pitching_stats <- uclapitching |> 
  group_by(NAME) |> 
  summarise(
   Strikeouts = sum(SO),
   Walks = sum(BB))

ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=pitching_stats, 
    aes(
      y=reorder(NAME, Strikeouts), yend=NAME,
      x=Strikeouts, xend=Walks),
    color = "grey",
    linewidth = 1.5

  ) +
  geom_point(
    data=pitching_stats,
    aes(
      y=NAME,
      x=Strikeouts,
      color="Strikeouts"
    ),
    size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=pitching_stats,
    aes(
      y=NAME,
      x=Walks,
    color="Walks"
    ),
    size=2
  )+ 
  scale_color_manual(name=NULL, values=c("grey", "black")) +
  new_scale_color() +
  geom_point(
    data=neb_pitching_stats,
    aes(
      y=NAME,
      x=Strikeouts,
      color="Strikeouts"
    ),
    size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=neb_pitching_stats,
    aes(
      y=NAME,
      x=Walks,
    color="Walks"
    ),
    size=2
  )+ 
  scale_color_manual(name=NULL, values=c("#e41c38", "black")) +
  new_scale_color() +
  geom_point(
    data=ucla_pitching_stats,
    aes(
      y=NAME,
      x=Strikeouts,
      color="Strikeouts"
    ),
    size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=ucla_pitching_stats,
    aes(
      y=NAME,
      x=Walks,
    color="Walks"
    ),
    size=2
  )+ 
  scale_color_manual(name=NULL, values=c("#2774AE", "black")) +
  labs(
    x="Walks (black) in relation to strikeouts (red/blue)", 
    y="Pitcher from Nebraska/UCLA based on color", 
    title="UCLA seems to have dominate pitching.", 
    subtitle="When looking at how the red of Nebraska falls behind the blue of UCLA, it seems \n obvious that the Bruins take the top spot by far; but how consistent are they?", 
    caption="Source: NCAA | By Marissa Monico"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot", 
    legend.position = "none"
    )

Logan Reddmann. That is all there is to say. He is the top pitcher in the NCAA right now. His walk-to-strike ratio is unheard of and every other pitcher wishes he has the attraction to the strike zone that Reddmann has. His 18-strikeout game against Rutgers will go down in the Bruin history books. He takes the top spot, obviously, but after more observation, Nebraska holds the next four spots. Our four starters throughout the year put distance between UCLA’s No. 1 and their number two. Jasa is biting at the heels of Reddmann’s strikeout number, while Katskee has almost thrown the same amount of walks. Every starting Husker pitcher that would face the Bruin batters is right up there with Reddmann. Seems like a big testament to their consistency. The other two weekend starters for UCLA remain at the bottom of the graph. Interesting.

Comparing the top two teams in the B1G can tell us a lot, but we need to also know about the success in the past two years that got Husker Baseball to where they are today. The bats have been on fire this year with multiple grand slams and at least one home run every game by Jett Buck or Drew Grego. How did that impact effect the past two Nebraska teams in their standings? Let’s find out.

Code 
hr26 <- read_csv("2026 HR - Sheet1.csv") |> separate(`W-L`, into=c("Win", "Loss"), sep="-") |> separate(`Team`, into=c("Team", "Conference"), sep="\\(") |> filter(Conference == "Big Ten)")
hr25 <- read_csv("2025 HR - Sheet1.csv") |> separate(`W-L`, into=c("Win", "Loss"), sep="-") |> separate(`Team`, into=c("Team", "Conference"), sep="\\(") |> filter(Conference == "Big Ten)")
hr24 <- read_csv("2024 HR - Sheet1.csv") |> separate(`W-L`, into=c("Win", "Loss"), sep="-") |> separate(`Team`, into=c("Team", "Conference"), sep="\\(") |> filter(Conference == "Big Ten)")

nu26 <- hr26 |> filter(Rank == "83")
nu25 <- hr25 |> filter(Rank == "126")
nu24 <- hr24 |> filter(Win == "40")

 
ggplot()+
  geom_bar(data=hr24, aes(x=reorder(Team, HR), weight=HR)) + 
  geom_bar(data=nu24, aes(x=reorder(Team, HR), weight=HR), fill="red")+
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="B1G team", 
    y="Number of home runs hit", 
    title="Nebraska Baseball was hitting barrells in 2024.", 
    subtitle="Out of all three seasons, Nebraska hit the most home runs, with just under 75.", 
    caption="Source: NCAA | By Marissa Monico"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot", 
    legend.position = "none"
    )
Code 
ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=hr25, aes(x=reorder(Team, HR), weight=HR)) + 
  geom_bar(data=nu25, aes(x=reorder(Team, HR), weight=HR), fill="red")+
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="B1G team", 
    y="Number of home runs hit", 
    title="Based off home runs, Nebraska should not \n have won the B1G Tournament in 2025.", 
    subtitle="The team was hitting below average, especially compared to their championship opponent, UCLA", 
    caption="Source: NCAA | By Marissa Monico"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot", 
    legend.position = "none"
    )
Code 
ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=hr26, aes(x=reorder(Team, HR), weight=HR)) + 
  geom_bar(data=nu26, aes(x=reorder(Team, HR), weight=HR), fill="red")+
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="B1G team", 
    y="Number of home runs hit", 
    title="In 2026, Nebraska Baseball stands above \n average in home runs, barely.", 
    subtitle="Although they sit above several teams, they are not hitting enough home runs compared to UCLA or Oregon.", 
    caption="Source: NCAA | By Marissa Monico"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot", 
    legend.position = "none"
    )

There is a lot to sift through here. In 2024, Nebraska was never in a better condition. They were fifth overall on the season, with just below 75 total home runs. 2025 was the complete opposite. They were actually sixth to last. Wait, but that graph looks different. Here is why: on August 4, 2024, UCLA, Oregon, USC, Washington, Indiana and a few other teams officially joined the B1G conference. That is why Nebraska was so high in 2024, and why they were so low in 2025. New powerhouses were introduced. Throughout the 41 games played in this current season, Nebraska has an okay showing, but UCLA and Oregon are sending balls into the parking lot game after game. That is not something that Nebraska can compete with at the moment.

So, what is to make of this? Nebraska has the consistent hitting and pitching to take the B1G Tournament title right out of UCLA’s hands, but do they have the power hitters? In my opinion, a consistent team always pulls through. We will find out next month.

item.title: Is is possible for one player to carry the whole program? 2023 Nebraska women’s soccer is the perfect team to look at.
item.dc:creator: Marissa Monico
item.description:
Nebraska sports in general are, let us face it, the poster-children for disappointment. Whenever it seems like a sport is coming close to a National Championship, or any sort of big win, our cover is blown. We have seen it with volleyball, football and now basketball. Not to say we should not be proud of these sports and their accomplishments, but it is hard to believe good things can happen to Nebraska Athletics.

So, when the womens soccer team won the B1G tournament in 2023, teary-eyed Nebraska fans started to hope this could mean something for a program that was not known for its success. That hope crumpled as the 2024 team lost game after game, plumetting to the bottom of the B1G at one point, then settling in a below average position.

Why is that?

The team had almost the same roster as the year before, had a good recruiting off-season and had all the momentum going into 2024. What changed?

Code 
library(tidyverse)
── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.2.0     ✔ readr     2.1.6
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.2     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.1     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
Code 
teamgoals2324 <- read_csv("teamgoals2324.csv") |> mutate(Season = "2023-2024")
Rows: 300 Columns: 7
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (2): Team, W-L
dbl (5): Rank, Team Games, Goals, GA, Diff

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
Code 
teamgoals2425 <- read_csv("teamgoals2425.csv") |> mutate(Season = "2024-2025")
Rows: 306 Columns: 7
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (2): Team, W-L
dbl (5): Rank, Team Games, Goals, GA, Diff

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
Code 
teamgoals <- bind_rows(teamgoals2324, teamgoals2425)

teamgoals <- teamgoals |> 
  separate(Team, into=c("Team", "Conference"), sep=" \\(") |> 
  mutate(Conference = gsub(")", "", Conference))
Warning: Expected 2 pieces. Additional pieces discarded in 10 rows [121, 130, 228, 245,
288, 392, 444, 462, 502, 510].
Code 
big10goals <- teamgoals |> filter(Conference == "Big Ten")

labels <- big10goals |> filter(Team == "Nebraska" | Team == "Penn St.")

ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data=big10goals, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Team), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=big10goals, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Team), color="grey") +
  geom_line(data=labels, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Team, color=Team)) +
  geom_point(data=labels, aes(x=Season, y=Goals, group=Team, color=Team)) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("red", "darkblue")) +
  labs(
    x="Seasons", 
    y="Goals scored per year", 
    title="Nebraska women's soccer fell off, hard.", 
    subtitle="Between the years of the 2023, and 2024 season, the Huskers scored significantly \n less goals while every other B1G team maintained some consistency.", 
    caption="Source: NCAA | By Marissa Monico"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

The slope between the two seasons is dramatic. While teams like Penn State stayed about even, or others had a slight uptick, only one team took a noticeable loss: Nebraska. The team scored 67 goals in the 2023-2024 season and 24 in the next. That is +38 down to -7.

Obviously, there was a big contributor that is missing. What if it had nothing to do with the offense? What if it was the defense that needs to be looked at?

Code 
goals2324 <- big10goals |> 
    filter(Season == "2023-2024") |>
  group_by(Team) |> 
  summarise(
   goals23 = sum(Goals),
   ga23 = sum(GA))

goals2425 <- big10goals |> 
    filter(Season == "2024-2025") |>
  group_by(Team) |> 
  summarise(
   goals24 = sum(Goals),
   ga24 = sum(GA))

ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=goals2324, 
    aes(
      y=Team, yend=Team,
      x=goals23, xend=ga23),
    color = "grey",
    linewidth = 1.5
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=goals2324,
    aes(
      y=Team,
      x=goals23,
      color="Goals"
    ),
    size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=goals2324,
    aes(
      y=Team,
      x=ga23,
    color="Goals Against"
    ),
    size=2
  )+ 
  scale_color_manual(name=NULL, values=c("red", "black")) +
  labs(
    x="Goals scored against in relation to goals scored in 2023-2024", 
    y="Team", 
    title="2023-2024 Nebraska soccer is ahead of the pack.", 
    subtitle="In the 2023-2024 season, the Huskers dominated the Big 10 in goals scored, surpassing \n every other team by a considerable margin and doubled the amount of goals scored against them.", 
    caption="Source: NCAA | By Marissa Monico"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

Nebraska is sitting with 29 goals scored against in the 2023 season, in relation to their 67 goals scored. They put the rest of the B1G to shame. What does the damage look like in 2024?

Code 
ggplot() + geom_segment( data=goals2425, aes( y=Team, yend=Team, x=goals24, xend=ga24), color="grey", linewidth = 1.5 ) + geom_point( data=goals2425, aes( y=Team, x=goals24, color="Goals" ), size=2 ) + geom_point( data=goals2425, aes( y=Team, x=ga24, color="Goals Against" ), size=2 )+ scale_color_manual(name=NULL, values=c("red", "black")) +
  labs(
    x="Goals scored against in relation to goals scored in 2024-2025", 
    y="Team", 
    title="Nebraska women's soccer drops into mediocrity.", 
    subtitle="In the following season, while goals against stayed within the same area, goals scored dropped significantly.", 
    caption="Source: NCAA | By Marissa Monico"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

The difference is so vast that Nebraska gets lost in the data. In reality, they only allowed two more goals than the year before, 31. However, that does not matter when you cannot score goals.

Defense does not seem to be the problem either. The goaltending was solid, and the back line was virtually the same as the year before.

So, what caused Nebraska women’s soccer downfall? What was the factor?

Eleanor Dale.

Code 
library(ggbeeswarm)
library(ggrepel)

topgoals <- read_csv("/Applications/spmc350/data/topgoals.csv")
Rows: 168 Columns: 10
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (4): Player, Cl, Ht, Pos
dbl (6): Rank, Games, Goals, Assists, Points, Per Game

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
Code 
nu <- topgoals |> 
  filter(Player == "Eleanor Dale, Nebraska (Big Ten)")

ggplot() + 
  geom_beeswarm(
    data=topgoals, 
    aes(x=Goals, y=Points), color="grey") + 
  geom_beeswarm(
    data=nu, 
    aes(x=Goals, y=Points), color="red")+ 
  geom_text_repel(
    data=nu, 
    aes(x=Goals, y=Points, label=Player)) +
  labs(
    x="Goals scored in the 2023-2024 season", 
    y="Points acquired in the 2023-2024 season", 
    title="Eleanor Dale rises to the top.", 
    subtitle="Out of all goal scored in the NCAA during the 2023-2024 season, Dale scored the most \n goals while securing the second-most amount of points, proving her domination.", 
    caption="Source: NCAA | By Marissa Monico"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

Dale was the leading goal scorer in all of the NCAA during the 2023 season, with 28 respectively. That is four more than the whole team managed to put up in the following season. Sarah Weber, the teams second-leading goal scorer had 16 in 2023, then 11 in 2024.

The absence of Dale created waves throughout the Nebraska offense that the rest of the team could not cover. Goals win games. So, when you cannot score, you cannot win.

Dale now plays overseas for Sunderland, where she is a striker, continuing her dominance on the pitch.
